trajectory.thought: The PCA scatter plot shows these groupings: (C1, C3), (T1, T3), (T4, T5, C6), (T2, C5, C6), (T6, C2, C4). Looking at the pattern, samples from the same patient number tend to cluster together regardless of treatment status — patients 1 and 3 appear in both C and T clusters separately, while patients 4/5/6 and 2/4/6 mix across treatment groups. The key observation is that treatment (T vs C) does not drive the clustering. Instead, samples group by some other factor — likely the batch or run in which they were processed. This is the hallmark of batch effects: technical variation that overshadows biological signal in PCA.

The PCA groupings show mixed treatment status within clusters: (C1, C3) are both controls but same patients as (T1, T3); other clusters mix treated and untreated from different patients. The clustering does not separate by treatment (T vs C) but instead groups samples that were likely processed together or share technical batch characteristics. This is the hallmark of batch effects — technical variation dominating over biological signal. For the second part, visualizing diverse cell types in scRNA-seq data with ~100,000 cells is best done with UMAP or t-SNE after clustering.

The PCA clustering pattern shows that samples cluster by patient identity rather than treatment status — treated and untreated samples from the same patients (or same batch) cluster together. This is the hallmark of **batch effects**: technical variation introduced by how/when samples were processed, rather than the biological variable of interest (drug treatment). The term for this effect is "batch effects."
The plan is complete. The PCA groupings show samples clustering by patient identity rather than treatment status (batch effects). For visualizing cell types in scRNA-seq, UMAP is the standard method.
